title: GORD
oneLiner: Common condition where gastric contents reflux into the oesophagus causing heartburn/regurgitation; may cause oesophagitis, strictures or Barrett's oesophagus.

sections.GORD (Gastro-Oesophageal Reflux Disease): **One-liner:** Common condition where gastric contents reflux into the oesophagus causing heartburn/regurgitation; may cause oesophagitis, strictures or Barrett's oesophagus.
sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS):** single finding – chest pain with ECG changes (ST elevation/depression) or raised troponin; do not dismiss as "heartburn" in at-risk patients.
- **Oesophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome):** single finding – subcutaneous emphysema or mediastinal crunch + severe vomiting/retching; get urgent CXR (look for pneumomediastinum/pleural effusion) and call surgical team immediately.
- **Severe oesophagitis with perforation/bleeding:** haematemesis, melaena, haemodynamic instability; suspect if epigastric pain + tachycardia + hypotension.
sections.Presentation:
- Cardinal symptoms: retrosternal burning (heartburn), acid regurgitation (especially after meals or lying down), dysphagia (intermittent, to solids), water brash (excess saliva), epigastric pain
- Key exam findings: often normal; may have epigastric tenderness, halitosis, dental erosions (chronic reflux)
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **ACS** – chest pain radiates to arm/jaw, exertional, ECG/troponin abnormal; **splitter:** pain that is positional/relieved by antacids suggests GORD, not heart
- **Peptic ulcer disease (PUD)** – epigastric pain with meals, melaena, H.pylori positive; **splitter:** if pain is solely retrosternal and not in epigastrium, think GORD
- **Oesophageal spasm** – dysphagia for both solids and liquids, chest pain may mimic angina; responds to nitrates; **splitter:** barium swallow shows "corkscrew" oesophagus
- **Eosinophilic oesophagitis** – dysphagia, food impaction, atopy (asthma/eczema), younger age; **splitter:** no response to standard PPI therapy; OGD shows furrows/rings
- **Oesophageal/gastric malignancy** – progressive dysphagia, weight loss, age >55, anorexia; **splitter:** alarm symptoms (see below) should prompt urgent OGD
- **Atypical chest pain** (costochondritis, pericarditis) – localised tenderness, pericardial rub
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** ECG (rule out ACS) – do before any treatment if chest pain present; pH/alginates trial (if symptoms resolve, supports GORD)
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia from chronic bleeding), U&Es (if vomiting), LFTs, troponin (if cardiac suspected)
- **Imaging:** no imaging required for uncomplicated GORD; CXR if perforation/ACS suspected (look for free air, mediastinal widening)
- **Special tests:** Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) – indicated if alarm symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, anaemia, age>55, vomiting, family history of oesophageal cancer). Manometry/oesophageal pH monitoring for atypical reflux or pre-op assessment
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis (symptom response to PPI trial) – positive response supports GORD; NICE recommends 4-week PPI trial if no alarm symptoms
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** in any patient >40 presenting with "heartburn" or chest pain – omission may miss ACS
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – If cardiac excluded, give antacid (e.g. Gaviscon Advance 10ml orally)  
  – If severe pain or dysphagia, IV PPI (pantoprazole 40mg IV or omeprazole 40mg IV)  
  – Rule out perforation (call surgical reg if surgical abdomen, tachycardia, high NEWS)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Usually not admitted unless complications (bleeding, perforation, stricture)  
  – If admitted: IV PPI, NBM till OGD if dysphagia, correct anaemia (IV iron/transfuse if Hb <70)  
  – Start oral PPI once swallowing safe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Lifestyle: weight loss, head of bed elevation (6-8 inches), avoid large meals before lying down, stop smoking, reduce alcohol, avoid trigger foods (fatty/spicy, chocolate, caffeine)  
  – PPI for 4-8 weeks (omeprazole 20mg od or lansoprazole 30mg od), then step-down to lowest effective dose  
  – If refractory: try twice-daily PPI or switch to different PPI; consider prokinetic (domperidone 10mg tds – caution QTc) or H2RA (ranitidine 150mg bd) as add-on  
  – Referrals: to gastroenterology if alarm symptoms, failed medical therapy, or suspected Barrett’s; to surgery if anti-reflux surgery considered
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – Fluids: 0.9% saline 1L/8h if NBM, check U&Es; otherwise oral hydration  
  – VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC od (adjust for weight/renal function)  
  – Diet: soft diet if dysphagia; NBM if planned OGD or acute bleeding  
  – Observations: 4-hourly NEWS signs, monitor for haematemesis/melaena
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (oral):**  
  – **Omeprazole** 20mg once daily (4-8 weeks) – oral or IV (40mg IV once daily for acute)  
  – **Lansoprazole** 30mg once daily (4-8 weeks) – oral  
  Alternative: **Esomeprazole** 20mg od (more potent), **Pantoprazole** 40mg od  
- **For breakthrough symptoms:** **Gaviscon Advance** 10ml after meals and at bedtime  
- **Contraindications/interactions:** PPIs may reduce clopidogrel efficacy (controversial; avoid omeprazole if clopidogrel – use pantoprazole instead); long-term PPI associated with C. diff infection, hip fracture, CKD – use lowest effective dose; domperidone – prolonged QTc, avoid if QTc >450ms or with interacting drugs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for PPIs in simple CKD; in severe hepatic impairment, consider reduced dose (omeprazole max 20mg daily). Domperidone – reduce dose in moderate-severe hepatic impairment; caution in renal (prolonged half-life)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing a PPI without stopping it – leading to long-term overuse** (increased risk of pneumonia, osteoporosis, C. diff, interstitial nephritis, and B12 deficiency). Also: **giving IV PPI when oral is feasible** (increases cost, risk of thrombophlebitis, no clinical benefit unless reduced consciousness or severe bleeding)
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:** Oesophagitis (erosion/ulcer), oesophageal stricture (dysphagia), bleeding (haematemesis/melaena), perforation (rare – surgical emergency)  
- **Chronic:** Barrett's oesophagus (metaplasia – risk of adenocarcinoma), oesophageal adenocarcinoma (screen if Barrett's), peptic stricture, pulmonary aspiration (chronic cough, pneumonia), laryngitis
sections.Prognosis: - Usually good – symptoms controlled with lifestyle ± PPI; 70% relapse within 6 months of stopping PPI, so long-term maintenance often needed. Barrett's has 0.5% annual risk of progression to cancer.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- Always **ask about alarm symptoms** (dysphagia, weight loss, anaemia, haematemesis, age >55, family history) before starting PPI – if present, refer for OGD within 2 weeks
- **Recommend head-of-bed elevation** (place blocks under bed posts) – reduces nocturnal reflux significantly
- **Check for H. pylori** if patient has PUD symptoms; treat if positive (confers lower recurrence of PUD but not GORD)
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Don’t ignore cardiac chest pain** – get ECG in all patients with retrosternal symptoms, especially if risk factors (MI history, diabetes, smoking, hypertension)
- **Don’t prescribe long-term PPI without clear indication** – plan to step-down or discontinue after 4-8 weeks; if ongoing symptoms refer to GI
- **Don’t give prokinetics** (domperidone/metoclopramide) if suspect obstruction or perforation – risk of dystonic reactions, GI rupture
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:** difficulty or pain on swallowing, unexplained weight loss, vomiting blood/coffee-grounds, black/tarry stools, chest pain not relieved by antacids
- **Follow-up:** GP for medication review and lifestyle advice in 4 weeks; if alarm symptoms present, urgent gastroenterology OGD appointment (2-week wait)
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- Call senior/ICU if:  
  – NEWS2 ≥5 or lactic acidosis (>2 mmol/L)  
  – Suspected perforation (sudden severe epigastric pain, peritonism, free air on CXR) – call surgical reg immediately  
  – Active severe upper GI bleed (ongoing haematemesis, haemodynamic instability, Hb drop >20g/L) – call GI bleed on-call team; consider ICU for instability  
  – Any chest pain with ECG changes or raised troponin – call cardiology/medical reg

rawMarkdown:
## GORD (Gastro-Oesophageal Reflux Disease)

**One-liner:** Common condition where gastric contents reflux into the oesophagus causing heartburn/regurgitation; may cause oesophagitis, strictures or Barrett's oesophagus.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Acute Coronary Syndrome (ACS):** single finding – chest pain with ECG changes (ST elevation/depression) or raised troponin; do not dismiss as "heartburn" in at-risk patients.
- **Oesophageal rupture (Boerhaave syndrome):** single finding – subcutaneous emphysema or mediastinal crunch + severe vomiting/retching; get urgent CXR (look for pneumomediastinum/pleural effusion) and call surgical team immediately.
- **Severe oesophagitis with perforation/bleeding:** haematemesis, melaena, haemodynamic instability; suspect if epigastric pain + tachycardia + hypotension.

### Presentation
- Cardinal symptoms: retrosternal burning (heartburn), acid regurgitation (especially after meals or lying down), dysphagia (intermittent, to solids), water brash (excess saliva), epigastric pain
- Key exam findings: often normal; may have epigastric tenderness, halitosis, dental erosions (chronic reflux)

### Differential Diagnosis
- **ACS** – chest pain radiates to arm/jaw, exertional, ECG/troponin abnormal; **splitter:** pain that is positional/relieved by antacids suggests GORD, not heart
- **Peptic ulcer disease (PUD)** – epigastric pain with meals, melaena, H.pylori positive; **splitter:** if pain is solely retrosternal and not in epigastrium, think GORD
- **Oesophageal spasm** – dysphagia for both solids and liquids, chest pain may mimic angina; responds to nitrates; **splitter:** barium swallow shows "corkscrew" oesophagus
- **Eosinophilic oesophagitis** – dysphagia, food impaction, atopy (asthma/eczema), younger age; **splitter:** no response to standard PPI therapy; OGD shows furrows/rings
- **Oesophageal/gastric malignancy** – progressive dysphagia, weight loss, age >55, anorexia; **splitter:** alarm symptoms (see below) should prompt urgent OGD
- **Atypical chest pain** (costochondritis, pericarditis) – localised tenderness, pericardial rub

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** ECG (rule out ACS) – do before any treatment if chest pain present; pH/alginates trial (if symptoms resolve, supports GORD)
- **Bloods:** FBC (anaemia from chronic bleeding), U&Es (if vomiting), LFTs, troponin (if cardiac suspected)
- **Imaging:** no imaging required for uncomplicated GORD; CXR if perforation/ACS suspected (look for free air, mediastinal widening)
- **Special tests:** Oesophagogastroduodenoscopy (OGD) – indicated if alarm symptoms (dysphagia, weight loss, anaemia, age>55, vomiting, family history of oesophageal cancer). Manometry/oesophageal pH monitoring for atypical reflux or pre-op assessment
- **Diagnostic criteria:** Clinical diagnosis (symptom response to PPI trial) – positive response supports GORD; NICE recommends 4-week PPI trial if no alarm symptoms
- **Never-miss test:** **ECG** in any patient >40 presenting with "heartburn" or chest pain – omission may miss ACS

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  – If cardiac excluded, give antacid (e.g. Gaviscon Advance 10ml orally)  
  – If severe pain or dysphagia, IV PPI (pantoprazole 40mg IV or omeprazole 40mg IV)  
  – Rule out perforation (call surgical reg if surgical abdomen, tachycardia, high NEWS)
- **Ward (first 24-72h):**  
  – Usually not admitted unless complications (bleeding, perforation, stricture)  
  – If admitted: IV PPI, NBM till OGD if dysphagia, correct anaemia (IV iron/transfuse if Hb <70)  
  – Start oral PPI once swallowing safe
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  – Lifestyle: weight loss, head of bed elevation (6-8 inches), avoid large meals before lying down, stop smoking, reduce alcohol, avoid trigger foods (fatty/spicy, chocolate, caffeine)  
  – PPI for 4-8 weeks (omeprazole 20mg od or lansoprazole 30mg od), then step-down to lowest effective dose  
  – If refractory: try twice-daily PPI or switch to different PPI; consider prokinetic (domperidone 10mg tds – caution QTc) or H2RA (ranitidine 150mg bd) as add-on  
  – Referrals: to gastroenterology if alarm symptoms, failed medical therapy, or suspected Barrett’s; to surgery if anti-reflux surgery considered
- **Default Orders (if admitted):**  
  – Fluids: 0.9% saline 1L/8h if NBM, check U&Es; otherwise oral hydration  
  – VTE prophylaxis: enoxaparin 40mg SC od (adjust for weight/renal function)  
  – Diet: soft diet if dysphagia; NBM if planned OGD or acute bleeding  
  – Observations: 4-hourly NEWS signs, monitor for haematemesis/melaena

### Drugs & Doses
- **First-line agents (oral):**  
  – **Omeprazole** 20mg once daily (4-8 weeks) – oral or IV (40mg IV once daily for acute)  
  – **Lansoprazole** 30mg once daily (4-8 weeks) – oral  
  Alternative: **Esomeprazole** 20mg od (more potent), **Pantoprazole** 40mg od  
- **For breakthrough symptoms:** **Gaviscon Advance** 10ml after meals and at bedtime  
- **Contraindications/interactions:** PPIs may reduce clopidogrel efficacy (controversial; avoid omeprazole if clopidogrel – use pantoprazole instead); long-term PPI associated with C. diff infection, hip fracture, CKD – use lowest effective dose; domperidone – prolonged QTc, avoid if QTc >450ms or with interacting drugs
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:** No dose adjustment needed for PPIs in simple CKD; in severe hepatic impairment, consider reduced dose (omeprazole max 20mg daily). Domperidone – reduce dose in moderate-severe hepatic impairment; caution in renal (prolonged half-life)
- **Most dangerous dose error:** **Prescribing a PPI without stopping it – leading to long-term overuse** (increased risk of pneumonia, osteoporosis, C. diff, interstitial nephritis, and B12 deficiency). Also: **giving IV PPI when oral is feasible** (increases cost, risk of thrombophlebitis, no clinical benefit unless reduced consciousness or severe bleeding)

### Complications
- **Acute:** Oesophagitis (erosion/ulcer), oesophageal stricture (dysphagia), bleeding (haematemesis/melaena), perforation (rare – surgical emergency)  
- **Chronic:** Barrett's oesophagus (metaplasia – risk of adenocarcinoma), oesophageal adenocarcinoma (screen if Barrett's), peptic stricture, pulmonary aspiration (chronic cough, pneumonia), laryngitis

### Prognosis
- Usually good – symptoms controlled with lifestyle ± PPI; 70% relapse within 6 months of stopping PPI, so long-term maintenance often needed. Barrett's has 0.5% annual risk of progression to cancer.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- Always **ask about alarm symptoms** (dysphagia, weight loss, anaemia, haematemesis, age >55, family history) before starting PPI – if present, refer for OGD within 2 weeks
- **Recommend head-of-bed elevation** (place blocks under bed posts) – reduces nocturnal reflux significantly
- **Check for H. pylori** if patient has PUD symptoms; treat if positive (confers lower recurrence of PUD but not GORD)

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Don’t ignore cardiac chest pain** – get ECG in all patients with retrosternal symptoms, especially if risk factors (MI history, diabetes, smoking, hypertension)
- **Don’t prescribe long-term PPI without clear indication** – plan to step-down or discontinue after 4-8 weeks; if ongoing symptoms refer to GI
- **Don’t give prokinetics** (domperidone/metoclopramide) if suspect obstruction or perforation – risk of dystonic reactions, GI rupture

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:** difficulty or pain on swallowing, unexplained weight loss, vomiting blood/coffee-grounds, black/tarry stools, chest pain not relieved by antacids
- **Follow-up:** GP for medication review and lifestyle advice in 4 weeks; if alarm symptoms present, urgent gastroenterology OGD appointment (2-week wait)

### Escalation Criteria
- Call senior/ICU if:  
  – NEWS2 ≥5 or lactic acidosis (>2 mmol/L)  
  – Suspected perforation (sudden severe epigastric pain, peritonism, free air on CXR) – call surgical reg immediately  
  – Active severe upper GI bleed (ongoing haematemesis, haemodynamic instability, Hb drop >20g/L) – call GI bleed on-call team; consider ICU for instability  
  – Any chest pain with ECG changes or raised troponin – call cardiology/medical reg
